section: Introduction, Results Overview, and Evaluation of Data Property Estimation
text: ARTICLE OPEN. A benchmark study of simulation methods for single-cell RNA sequencing data. Yue Cao1,2, Pengyi Yang 1,2,3,4& Jean Yee Hwa Yang 1,2,4. ABSTRACT: Single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) data simulation is critical for evaluating computational methods for analysing scRNA-seq data especially when ground truth is experimentally unattainable. The reliability of evaluation depends on the ability of simulation methods to capture properties of experimental data. However, while many scRNA-seq data simulation methods have been proposed, a systematic evaluation of these methods is lacking. Here, we develop a comprehensive evaluation framework, SimBench, including a kernel density estimation measure to benchmark 12 simulation methods through 35 scRNA-seq experimental datasets. We evaluate the simulation methods on a panel of data properties, ability to maintain biological signals, scalability and applicability. Our benchmark uncovers performance differences among the methods and highlights the varying difficulties in simulating data characteristics. Furthermore, we identify several limitations including maintaining heterogeneity of distribution. These results, together with the framework and datasets made publicly available as R packages, will guide simulation methods selection and their future development. INTRODUCTION: Single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) is a powerful technique for profiling the transcriptomes at the single-cell resolution and has gained considerable popularity. To effectively utilise scRNA-seq data to address biological questions, the development of computational tools for analysing such data is critical. Evaluation of their performance with credible ground truth has thus become a key task. In silico simulation methods for creating scRNA-seq datasets with desired structure and ground truth (e.g. number of cell groups) are an effective and practical strategy for evaluating computational tools. Numerous scRNA-seq data simulation methods have been developed, most employing a two-step process: using statistical models to estimate characteristics of real experimental data and using the learnt information as a template to generate simulation data. The distinctive difference is the choice of underlying statistical framework. Early methods often employ negative binomial (NB) as it has been the typical choice for modelling gene expression count of RNA-seq. Its variant, zero-inflated NB (ZINB) model takes account of excessive zeros. Alternative models have been proposed to increase modelling flexibility including gamma-normal mixture model, beta-Poisson, gamma-multivariate hypergeometric and mixture of zero-inflated Poisson and log-normal Poisson distributions. Other studies proposed semi-parametric or deep learning-based approaches. A common challenge is the ability to generate data that faithfully reflect experimental data. Deviations can greatly affect the validity and generalisability of evaluation results. A thorough assessment is crucial and timely. RESULTS OVERVIEW: Our SimBench framework evaluates 12 recently published simulation methods. We curated 35 public scRNA-seq datasets that include major experimental protocols, tissue types, and organisms. To assess a method's performance, SimBench splits the data into input data and test data (real data). Simulation data is generated based on properties estimated from the input data and compared with the real data. We use four key sets of evaluation criteria. The first set, data property estimation, assesses how realistic is a given simulated data. We defined 13 distinct criteria capturing both distributions of genes and cells as well as higher-order interactions. We chose to use the kernel density based global two-sample comparison test statistic (KDE statistic) to quantify similarity. The other three sets assess each method's ability to maintain biological signals, computational scalability, and its applicability. For biological signals, we measured the proportion of differentially expressed (DE) genes and other gene signals. Scalability is measured through computational run time and memory usage. Applicability examines practical application in terms of simulating multiple cell groups and differential expression patterns. Our framework provides recommendations by considering all aspects. COMPARISON RESULTS: Through ranking the 12 methods, we found that no method clearly outperformed others across all criteria. We examine each set individually. For data property estimation, variability in methods' performance across the 13 criteria was observed. ZINB-WaVE, SPARSim and SymSim performed better than others across almost all 13 data properties. For the remaining methods, a greater discrepancy was observed. We observed that some methods (e.g. zingeR and scDesign) that were not ranked highest in data properties estimation performed well in retaining biological signals. scDesign is designed for power calculation, while zingeR is designed to evaluate DE detection; both require accurate simulation of biological signals. For computational scalability, the majority of methods showed good performance. However, some top performing methods, such as SPsimSeq and ZINB-WaVE revealed poor scalability, highlighting a potential trade-off between computational efficiency and modelling complexity. Different simulation methods satisfy different numbers of the applicability criteria, partly because not all are designed as general purpose simulation tools. For example, powsimR was designed for power analysis, while SPARSim, SymSim and Splat are general purpose tools.

section: Impact of Data Characteristics, Common Strengths/Weaknesses, Discussion, and Methods
text: IMPACT OF DATA CHARACTERISTICS: We examined the impact of data characteristics including cell numbers and sequencing protocols. Using subsamples of Tabula Muris data, regression analysis showed certain data properties were more accurately estimated with larger cell numbers (e.g., mean-variance relationships), but most other properties were negatively correlated with increasing cell number, suggesting maintaining properties may become more challenging with increasing data complexity. Examination of datasets with multiple protocols applied to the same sample revealed no substantial impact of protocol difference on overall simulation results. The choice of reference input being shallow or deep sequencing has no substantial impact. SymSim and powsimR are the only two methods requiring specification of input data as deep or shallow protocols; a general simulation framework for the two major classes may be sufficient. COMMON STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES: We identified criteria where most methods performed well or were lacking. Data properties relating to higher-order interactions (e.g., mean-variance relationship) revealed larger differences between simulated and real data, highlighting difficulty in incorporating them. Gene- and cell-wise properties such as fraction of zero per cell were more accurately captured. For biological signals, differentially distributed (DD) and differential proportion (DP) genes exhibited greater difference. Four out of 12 methods consistently had very low SMAPE scores, simulating close to zero proportions of biological signals irrespective of the true proportion. DISCUSSION: We presented a comprehensive benchmark assessing 12 methods using 35 datasets and 25 criteria. We highlighted recommendations for method selection and identified areas for improvement. We found various underlying models were used. Among top performers, ZINB-WaVE, SPARSim, SymSim, Splat, and scDesign use different frameworks. While deep learning methods have dominated various fields, cscGAN, a deep learning-based model, had only moderate performance. Maintaining a reasonable amount of biological signal is desirable and was not well captured by some methods. We also noted genes generated by some methods were assigned uninformative names, limiting assessment to proportion rather than specific gene preservation. Several simulation studies only assessed gene- and cell-wise properties, not examining higher-order interactions. We identified that none of the methods could maintain heterogeneity in cell population due to patient variability, as some methods require input to be a homogeneous population. There exist trade-offs between the four aspects of criteria; a well-rounded approach could be more important. A few methods (SERGIO, PROSSTT, dyngen, Lun's) were excluded due to unique properties requiring special inputs. Our evaluation using both limma and DEsingle shows high agreement in rankings. In conclusion, we have illustrated the usefulness of our framework. The evaluation framework is available as the R package SimBench. Datasets are available as SimBenchData. A Shiny application is provided. METHODS: Dataset collection: 35 publicly available datasets. Quality control performed. Tabula Muris used for scalability. 25 datasets with multiple cell types/conditions used for biological signals. Selection and implementation: 12 methods. Data split 50% training, 50% testing. Evaluation of data property estimation: 13 data properties measured. KDE test used. Transformation applied: $x_{transformed} = \frac{|x| - |x_{minimum}|}{|x_{maximum}| - |x_{minimum}|}$. Other measures assessed: Average silhouette width, average local silhouette width, NN rejection fraction, K-S statistics, scaled area between eCDFs, Runs statistics. Multi-step score aggregation: Weighted sum by cell type proportion: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i * w_i)$. Median score across datasets. Overall rank by mean rank across criteria. Evaluation of biological signals: Six categories: DE(limma), DE(DEsingle), DV, DD, DP, BD. SMAPE used: $SMAPE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{|F_t - A_t|}{\frac{A_t + F_t}{2}}$. Evaluation of scalability: Tabula Muris dataset subset. Running time and memory measured. Impact of data characteristics: Linear regression: $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1$. ANOVA for protocol impact. Data and code availability: All datasets publicly available. SimBench and SimBenchData available. Shiny application available.
